question: what happened in the attack in the war-torn capital?
text:
(CNN)  -- At least nine people were killed in Somalia's war-torn capital Mogadishu when mortars slammed into a home for disabled veterans, according to journalists and witnesses.



Al Shabaab fighters display two bodies of Somali police officers during a battle August 12 in Mogadishu.



At least 27 people were wounded in the Friday night incident, when Muslim militants fired mortars toward Mogadishu's port and struck a residential area. The mortars hit a home for former national army officers who were disabled in a late-1970s war with Ethiopia, the sources said.



Insurgents from the Al-Shabaab militant group have been fighting to topple Somalia's government. Its fighters have frequently shelled the city's airport and seaport, which are controlled by the African Union and government forces.



The United States is supporting the Somali government's fight against the insurgents, including providing weapons to government forces. Al Shabaab is on the U.S. list of terror organizations because of its ties to Osama bin Laden's al Qaeda network.



The United States is concerned that Somalia's weak government could fall to the Islamist insurgency, as it did in 2006 before Ethiopian forces ousted the militants from power later that year.

question: Who found international fame with ''Farewell My Concubine''?
text:
(CNN) -- Director Chen Kaige's films often examine the close ties of love, passion and friendship, but his own relationship with his artist and filmmaker father is one that has informed much of his life and colored his work.





Chen Kaige captures the pains and passions of relationships on the silver screen.





As a 14-year-old red guard during the Cultural Revolution, Chen denounced his father for creating subversive art. While his father forgave him, Chen has found it hard to truly forgive himself.





"I knew it was the wrong thing to do. If I didn't know, then I could've forgiven myself," he told CNN.





Chen has already touched upon such a significant moment in his personal life in his award-winning 1993 film, "Farewell My Concubine." It brought him international-acclaim and a host of accolades, including Cannes Film Festival's Palme D'or.





Like "Farewell My Concubine," Chen's latest film, "Forever Enthralled" ("Mei Lanfang" in Mandarin), once again delves into the world of Beijing opera, and it has proven to be a hit in China since its release in cinemas in December.





However, capturing the Cultural Revolution remains one of Chen's ambitions -- not only to explore the circumstances that colored the relationship with his father but an exploration of a crucial time in Chinese history.





"It's not because I was hurt and so many people hurt that I want to do my revenge. It's not like this. This is the lesson that the Chinese people must learn, no matter what," Chen told CNN.





"I hope that my generation of directors or writers could do something to tell people the truth, to tell people what really happened at that time; why a young son of a film director, you know, could denounce his own father, like I did. I was still shocked," he said.





Now 56 years old, Chen studied at the Beijing Film Academy, directing his first feature film "Yellow Earth" in 1984.





After the success of "Farewell My Concubine," he eventually took up the offer to direct a Hollywood film in 2002 -- "Killing Me Softly," with Heather Graham and Joseph Fiennes.





While failing to impress the critics or register a hit at the box office, Chen remains sanguine about the experience.





"I think I've learnt a lot from that. Although I fully understand why audiences didn't really enjoy that film that much.





"In the industrial system [of Hollywood] people don't really care if you're making a good film or not. People care about whether the film can make money. It's a completely different thing, you know. Quite honestly, I wasn't very comfortable to do films that way," said Chen.





Chen will continue to make films in China and finds the country's recent transformation as significant as earlier periods of change during his lifetime.





"I ask myself whether it's a progress or we're just going back to where we not necessarily need to be. I don't know. I mean, I'm not trying to say anything politically. But I feel if you look at the very beginning of Charles Dickens' novel, 'A Tale of Two Cities,' 'It was the best times, it was the worst times...' this is the simple answer," he said.





Time will tell if today's China will be part of Chen's cinematic sweep in the future.

question: Who found international fame with ''Farewell My Concubine''?
text:
(CNN) -- Director Chen Kaige's films frequently explore the intricate connections of love, passion, and friendship; however, his own relationship with his father, an artist and filmmaker, has profoundly influenced his life and shaped his artistic endeavors.

Chen Kaige adeptly portrays the complexities and emotional struggles of relationships on the cinematic canvas.

As a 14-year-old Red Guard during the Cultural Revolution, Chen publicly denounced his father for producing subversive art. Although his father extended forgiveness, Chen has grappled with the challenge of forgiving himself.

"I recognized that it was the incorrect course of action. Had I been unaware, I might have been able to absolve myself," he conveyed to CNN.

Chen has previously addressed this pivotal moment in his personal history in his acclaimed 1993 film, "Farewell My Concubine," which garnered him international recognition and numerous accolades, including the prestigious Palme d'Or at the Cannes Film Festival.

Similar to "Farewell My Concubine," Chen's most recent film, "Forever Enthralled" ("Mei Lanfang" in Mandarin), once again immerses viewers in the realm of Beijing opera and has achieved considerable success in China since its theatrical release in December.

Nonetheless, capturing the essence of the Cultural Revolution remains one of Chen's aspirations—not solely to examine the dynamics that influenced his relationship with his father but also to investigate a pivotal period in Chinese history.

"My motivation is not rooted in personal grievances or a desire for retribution. Rather, it is imperative that the Chinese populace learns from this historical lesson, irrespective of the circumstances," Chen articulated to CNN.

"I aspire for my generation of directors and writers to convey the truth, to illuminate what transpired during that era; to elucidate why a young son of a film director, such as myself, could denounce his own father. I remain profoundly affected by this realization," he remarked.

Now at the age of 56, Chen pursued his studies at the Beijing Film Academy, directing his inaugural feature film, "Yellow Earth," in 1984.

Following the success of "Farewell My Concubine," he accepted an opportunity to direct a Hollywood film in 2002—"Killing Me Softly," featuring Heather Graham and Joseph Fiennes.

Despite failing to impress critics or achieve commercial success, Chen maintains a positive outlook regarding the experience.

"I believe I have gained significant insights from that endeavor, although I fully comprehend why audiences did not particularly enjoy that film.

"In the Hollywood industrial system, the focus is not necessarily on the quality of the film but rather on its profitability. It is an entirely different paradigm, and quite frankly, I found it uncomfortable to create films in that manner," Chen stated.

Chen intends to continue his filmmaking endeavors in China and perceives the country's recent transformations as equally significant as earlier periods of change throughout his lifetime.

"I often reflect on whether this represents progress or if we are merely reverting to a state that may not be beneficial. I am uncertain. I do not wish to make political statements; however, I am reminded of the opening lines of Charles Dickens' novel, 'A Tale of Two Cities': 'It was the best of times, it was the worst of times...' This encapsulates the essence of the situation," he expressed.

Time will reveal whether contemporary China will feature prominently in Chen's future cinematic narratives.

"It's not because I was hurt and so many people hurt that I want to do my revenge. It's not like this. "I hope that my generation of directors or writers could do something to tell people the truth, to tell people what really happened at that time; why a young son of a film director, you know, could denounce his own father, like I did. I was still shocked," he expressed, emphasizing his desire for a deeper understanding of the past among future storytellers.

Now at 56 years old, Chen's cinematic journey began at the Beijing Film Academy, where he honed his craft before making his directorial debut with "Yellow Earth" in 1984. Following the monumental success of "Farewell My Concubine," he ventured into Hollywood, directing "Killing Me Softly" in 2002, which starred Heather Graham and Joseph Fiennes. Despite the film's lukewarm reception from critics and audiences alike, Chen remains optimistic about the lessons gleaned from that experience. "I think I've learnt a lot from that. Although I fully understand why audiences didn't really enjoy that film that much," he reflected, acknowledging the stark contrasts between the Hollywood film industry and his own artistic values. He noted, "In the industrial system [of Hollywood], people don't really care if you're making a good film or not. People care about whether the film can make money. It's a completely different thing, you know. Quite honestly, I wasn't very comfortable to do films that way," he admitted.

Looking ahead, Chen plans to continue his filmmaking journey in China, where he perceives the recent transformations as significant as the historical changes he has witnessed throughout his life. "I ask myself whether it's a progress or we're just going back to where we not necessarily need to be. I don't know. I mean, I'm not trying to say anything politically. But I feel if you look at the very beginning of Charles Dickens' novel, 'A Tale of Two Cities,' 'It was the best times, it was the worst times...' this is the simple answer," he mused, reflecting on the complexities and dualities of contemporary China.

As time unfolds, it remains to be seen how the evolving landscape of today's China will influence Chen's future cinematic narratives and whether he will continue to intertwine the threads of his personal history with the broader cultural fabric of his films.
